questions.question: What is the primary purpose of React hooks?
options: To replace Redux entirely
To allow state and lifecycle features in functional components
To improve CSS styling in React apps
To enforce class-based component usage
questions.correctAnswer: To allow state and lifecycle features in functional components
questions.explanation: Hooks allow functional components to use state, side effects, context, refs, and other React features that previously required class components. This makes components easier to write, reuse, and test.

questions.question: What is the difference between useState and useReducer?
options: useState is for global state, useReducer is for local state
useReducer is better suited for complex state logic, while useState is simpler
useState is asynchronous while useReducer is synchronous
There is no difference between them
questions.correctAnswer: useReducer is better suited for complex state logic, while useState is simpler
questions.explanation: useState is ideal for simple pieces of state, while useReducer is better for managing complex state transitions or multiple related state values. It centralizes update logic in a reducer function, making it easier to maintain.

questions.question: What does the dependency array in useEffect control?
options: Which components can access the effect
When the effect runs based on changes to specified values
How many times the component re-renders
Whether the component uses state or props
questions.correctAnswer: When the effect runs based on changes to specified values
questions.explanation: The dependency array tells React when to re-run an effect. An empty array runs the effect once after the initial render, omitted dependencies run it after every render, and specified dependencies cause it to run only when those values change.

questions.question: What is the virtual DOM in React?
options: A direct representation of the real DOM
A lightweight copy of the real DOM used to optimize updates
A browser API for DOM manipulation
A replacement for HTML
questions.correctAnswer: A lightweight copy of the real DOM used to optimize updates
questions.explanation: The Virtual DOM is an in-memory representation of the UI. When state changes, React compares the new Virtual DOM with the previous one (a process called reconciliation) and updates only the necessary parts of the real DOM, improving performance.

questions.question: Why are keys important when rendering lists in React?
options: They improve CSS styling
They help React identify which elements have changed for efficient re-rendering
They allow components to share state
They are required for event handling
questions.correctAnswer: They help React identify which elements have changed for efficient re-rendering
questions.explanation: Keys give each list item a stable identity so React can efficiently determine which items were added, removed, or reordered. Using unique, stable keys helps preserve component state and minimizes unnecessary re-renders.

questions.question: What is prop drilling in React?
options: Passing props deeply through multiple components unnecessarily
Using props to manage global state
A method of optimizing component rendering
A way to fetch data from APIs
questions.correctAnswer: Passing props deeply through multiple components unnecessarily
questions.explanation: Prop drilling occurs when data must be passed through several intermediate components that don't actually use it, just so a deeply nested component can access it. Context or state management libraries are common solutions when this becomes excessive.

questions.question: What is the purpose of React.memo?
options: To memoize values inside a component
To prevent unnecessary re-renders of functional components based on props
To manage side effects
To handle asynchronous code
questions.correctAnswer: To prevent unnecessary re-renders of functional components based on props
questions.explanation: React.memo is a higher-order component that memoizes a functional component. If its props haven't changed, React can skip rendering it, improving performance in situations where rendering is expensive.

questions.question: What is the difference between controlled and uncontrolled components?
options: Controlled components manage state internally, uncontrolled rely on Redux
Controlled components use React state, uncontrolled components rely on the DOM
Uncontrolled components are deprecated
There is no difference
questions.correctAnswer: Controlled components use React state, uncontrolled components rely on the DOM
questions.explanation: A controlled component stores form values in React state and updates them with event handlers, making React the single source of truth. An uncontrolled component lets the DOM manage the value, typically accessed using refs.

questions.question: What does useContext help avoid?
options: Using hooks in functional components
Prop drilling by providing a way to share values across components
Component re-renders entirely
Using external libraries
questions.correctAnswer: Prop drilling by providing a way to share values across components
questions.explanation: useContext allows components to consume shared values directly from a Context provider without passing props through every intermediate component. This simplifies component hierarchies and reduces prop drilling.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of code splitting in React?
options: It reduces the number of components
It improves performance by loading code only when needed
It eliminates the need for state management
It simplifies JSX syntax
questions.correctAnswer: It improves performance by loading code only when needed
questions.explanation: Code splitting divides an application into smaller JavaScript bundles that are loaded on demand. This reduces the initial bundle size, speeds up page loads, and improves the user experience. React commonly supports this with React.lazy() and Suspense.
